1: vayim'alu vnei Yisrael ma'al bachareim: ‘the sons of Israel committed a trespass (ma'al) regarding the devoted things’ — ma'al is a technical term for breach of faith specifically regarding sacred things: misappropriating what belongs to YHWH (cf. Leviticus 5:15). What makes this verse theologically striking is that YHWH’s anger burned against Israel before the military defeat, before any human awareness of the sin. The hidden sin had already compromised the covenant relationship. Sin’s effects are not dependent on its discovery.
2: Joshua sends spies to Ai (ha-Ai, the ruin) — the same intelligence-gathering approach as Jericho. The city’s name (Ai = ruin) anticipates its fate: what is already a ruin will be made a permanent ruin after Achan’s sin is dealt with. The scouts recommend a limited force — the same self-confidence that failed at Kadesh (Numbers 14:40-45) when Israel presumed on divine favor without divine command. Human military assessment without divine consultation is the structural error that Jericho’s victory might have generated as a temptation.
3: al yaal sham kol haam: ‘not all the people need to go up’ — reasonable military calculation: why exhaust the whole army against a small target? But the calculation omits the variable that matters: YHWH’s presence is not automatic. The assumption that victory follows from previous victory without consulting YHWH is the error. This is the warning of Deuteronomy 32:15 — prosperity can generate the self-reliance that forfeits divine presence.
4: vayanusu mipnei anshei haAi: ‘they fled before the men of Ai’ — described with the same vocabulary used of Canaanite fear before Israel (Joshua 2:11; Joshua 5:1). Now the Israelites experience what they produced in their enemies: routed flight, melting hearts. The reversal is exact and painful. Israel defeated without YHWH is no different from any other nation; the divine presence, not ethnic identity or military prowess, was the only thing that made Israel different.
5: vayyimas levav haam vayhi lemayim: ‘the hearts of the people melted and became like water’ — this is the exact phrase Rahab used of Jericho (Joshua 2:11: vayimass levavchem) and Joshua used of Canaanites (Joshua 2:9). Israel is now experiencing what they inflicted on their enemies. The narrative symmetry is jarring: the people of God, living outside the covenant relationship, are as vulnerable to fear and defeat as the nations they were supposed to drive out.
6: vayiqra Yehoshua simlotav vayipol al panav artzah lifnei aron YHWH: ‘Joshua tore his garments and fell facedown before the ark of YHWH’ — mourning rites before the ark are appropriate responses to apparent divine abandonment, and they position Joshua correctly: before YHWH, seeking understanding. The elders pour dust on their heads — intensified mourning. The lament that follows is not faithless complaint but covenant-based questioning: YHWH brought us here; why would he abandon us? This is the form of Ps 44 and Lamentations — bold covenant appeal in the face of apparent divine failure.
7: lama he'avarta he'avir et ha'am hazeh et hayarden: ‘why did you bring this people across the Jordan at all?’ — Joshua’s lament echoes the wilderness generation’s complaint (Numbers 11:15; Joshua 14:3: “it would have been better to die in Egypt”). The similarity is deliberate: Joshua the leader, in his despair, sounds like the faithless people he replaced. Crisis produces regression to earlier complaints; even the greatest leaders can momentarily sound like those they lead.
8: Joshua’s second lament: what can I say after Israel has turned tail? The concern is for the divine reputation — if Israel is defeated, what will YHWH’s standing among the nations be? This is the same argument Moses used at the golden calf (Exodus 32:12). The concern for divine honor in the eyes of the nations is not political spin but theological conviction: YHWH’s name and Israel’s fate are linked — when Israel fails, YHWH’s faithfulness appears to fail with it.
9: mah ta'aseh leshimcha hagadol: ‘what will you do for your great name?’ — the appeal to divine self-interest is audacious but theologically precise: YHWH bound himself to Israel by covenant; Israel’s destruction would reflect on the covenant-maker. Moses and Joshua both understand that covenant prayer can appeal to YHWH’s own character and reputation. This is not manipulation but faith in the weight of the covenant bond.
10: qum lecha lamah zeh attah nophel al panecha: ‘Get up! Why are you lying on your face?’ — YHWH’s response is brisk: the time for lament is over; the problem has a solution; stop grieving and act. The divine call from lament to action appears in Elijah’s cave encounter (1 Kings 19:5) and in the NT: “awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you” (Ephesians 5:14). Mourning without moving is not faithfulness.
11: Yisrael chata vegam avru et briti asher tziviti otam: ‘Israel has sinned and transgressed my covenant that I commanded them’ — YHWH’s diagnosis is precise: the sin is corporate, it is a specific act (lakchu min hacherem, they took from the devoted things), and it is a covenant transgression. The corporate guilt for one man’s sin raises the theological problem of collective responsibility (cf. Numbers 16:22: “shall one man sin and you be angry at the whole congregation?”). The community had not built in accountability structures to prevent cherem violation.
12: lo tuchal bnei Yisrael laqum lifnei oyveihem: ‘Israel cannot stand against their enemies’ — the connection between covenant sin and military inability is explicit. The cherem that was to be kept outside Israel has come inside it through Achan’s act: Israel itself has become cherem (hayiti lecharaim). Sin makes the holy community its own enemy by importing into itself the thing it was commanded to destroy.
13: qum qadesh et haam: ‘arise, consecrate the people’ — YHWH’s first command is preparation: consecrate before the investigation. The holiness-before-process sequence is consistent throughout: Sinai’s consecration before the theophany (Exodus 19:10-11), the priests’ purification before ministry. Access to divine process requires preparation of the community.
14: veqaravtem laboqer leshivteihem: ‘in the morning you shall be presented by tribes’ — the investigative lot-process narrows from tribe to clan to household to individual. The procedure is meticulous: YHWH does not simply name the culprit but walks Israel through a process that makes the identification certain and impossible to dispute. The lot system is not randomness but divine revelation (Proverbs 16:33: “the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH”). Every level is exonerated by the process as it narrows.
15: venivlah asah beYisrael: ‘he has done folly (nevalah) in Israel’ — nevalah is a strong term typically associated with sexual immorality and sacrilege (Genesis 34:7; 2 Samuel 13:12). Its application to covenant theft is striking: taking the cherem is morally equivalent to sexual violation of the covenant community. The same category-mixing (sacrilege = violation) reveals how the OT understands the covenant as intimate relationship: breach of its terms is something close to sexual betrayal — hence the prophets’ repeated use of marital infidelity as the metaphor for idolatry.
16: The early-morning process begins: tribe by tribe before YHWH. The tribe of Judah is identified — the royal tribe from which the messianic line will come. The identification of the sinner within Judah does not taint the tribe permanently; David will come from Judah, and Achan’s sin does not disqualify Judah from its messianic vocation. Individual sin does not determine tribal or corporate identity eternally.
17: vayiqrev et mishpachat Yehudah vayilkod et mishpachat haZarchi: tribe (Judah), clan (Zerah), household (Zabdi), individual (Achan). The Zerah connection is pointed: Zerah was the twin son of Judah and Tamar (Genesis 38:30) whose birth was marked by a scarlet thread — echoing Rahab’s scarlet cord. In Judah’s line, scarlet threads mark both salvation (Rahab, Zerah) and now judgment (Achan of Zerah’s clan). The same family connection can carry either promise or condemnation depending on the individual’s faithfulness.
18: vayaqrev et beito lagvarim vayilkad Achan ben Carmi: ‘he brought his household forward man by man, and Achan was taken’ — the final identification. The name Achan is connected by the narrative to achar (trouble) in v25 and to the Valley of Achor. The lot falls on one man after careful elimination, creating a publicly witnessed, undeniable identification: no appeal is possible — the process itself was YHWH’s revelation.
19: ten na kavod laYHWH Elohei Yisrael veten lo todah: ‘give glory to YHWH God of Israel and praise him’ — Joshua’s invitation to confess is framed as an act of worship: confessing sin is giving glory to God, acknowledging that YHWH is just and his judgment is right. The same formula appears in John 9:24 (Pharisees demand to the healed blind man: “give glory to God”) and Revelation 14:7 (“give glory to him, for the hour of his judgment has come”). Confession as gloria is a consistent biblical structure.
20: omen anochi chatati laYHWH Elohei Yisrael: ‘it is true — I have sinned against YHWH God of Israel’ — the confession is direct and complete: omen anochi (truly it is I), chatati (I have sinned), addressed to YHWH. The sin is named theologically before it is described materially. This formal structure of confession — acknowledgment, theological framing, directed to God — is the model for genuine repentance throughout Scripture.
21: va'ere bish'al adderet Shinar achat tovah vachmadem va'eqqachem: ‘I saw… I coveted… I took’ — Achan’s account follows the same three-step sequence as the first sin in Eden: saw (vaere), coveted (vachmadem), took (va'eqqachem). The verb chamad (covet) is the exact word of the tenth commandment (Exodus 20:17: “you shall not covet”). Achan was not ignorant of the law; he performed the act prohibited in the commandment against coveting. The Babylonian cloak (adderet Shinar) — Shinar being Babylon — anticipates the Babylonian exile that will ultimately destroy what Jericho’s fall began.
22: Joshua’s men ran to the tent (vayarutzu, they ran) — urgency. Everything is exactly as Achan described: the cloak, the silver, the gold wedge, hidden in the tent with the silver underneath. The confession is verified by the evidence; the evidence matches the confession perfectly. The hidden things are found when sought: “be sure your sin will find you out” (Numbers 32:23) is the pattern operating here as throughout Scripture.
23: vayiqachem mittoch ha'ohel vayavium el Yehoshua: ‘they took the things from the tent and brought them to Joshua and all the Israelites’ — the public display of the hidden goods restores the contamination to visibility. The community must see what was hidden; the hidden sin must become public before the community can be restored. There is a consistent OT principle that sin’s communal consequences require communal disclosure and judgment, not just private dealing.
24: emek Achor: the transfer to the Valley of Achor (trouble) is precise: everything connected to the sin — the man, his household, his goods, and the goods he stole — is brought together for judgment. The naming of the valley creates a permanent memorial. Hosea later transforms this valley: “I will make the Valley of Achor a door of hope” (Hosea 2:15) — the place of Israel’s greatest corporate shame becomes the place of future restoration.
25: mah avar'tanu: ‘why have you troubled us?’ — the wordplay: akar (trouble) and emek Achor (Valley of Trouble). The name-to-fate technique creates a permanent linguistic memorial in the land’s geography. Achan’s execution by stoning and burning follows the cherem-contamination principle: what is contaminated by contact with cherem must be destroyed as cherem.
26: vayashov YHWH mechamat apo: ‘YHWH turned from his burning anger’ — the language of divine wrath and its turning is anthropopathic but structurally consistent: covenantal violation produces divine anger; covenantal justice produces the turning of that anger. The heap of stones over Achan creates a monument that will stand throughout Israel’s history — and the valley that marked sin’s judgment becomes, through divine grace, the threshold of restored covenant (Hosea 2:15).
